    {
      "codeBlock": {
        "detailedLines": [
          {
            "lineNumber": 366,
            "text": "// LEGACY DEACTIVATE SIM endpoint (for backward compatibility)"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 367,
            "text": "router.post('/deactivate/:transactionId', verifyJWT, async (req, res) =\u003e {"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 368,
            "text": "  try {"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 369,
            "text": "    const { uid } = req.user;"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 370,
            "text": "    const { transactionId } = req.params;"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 371
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 372,
            "text": "    const userDoc = await db.collection('users').doc(uid).get();"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 373,
            "text": "    const user = userDoc.data();"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 374
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 375,
            "text": "    // Find the SIM to deactivate"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 376,
            "text": "    const simToDeactivate = user.registeredSims?.find(sim =\u003e sim.transactionId === transactionId);"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 377,
            "text": "    if (!simToDeactivate) {"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 378,
            "text": "      return res.status(404).json({ error: 'SIM not found' });"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 379,
            "text": "    }"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 380
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 381,
            "text": "    // Update user's SIM list"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 382,
            "text": "    const updatedSims = user.registeredSims.map(sim =\u003e {"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 383,
            "text": "      if (sim.transactionId === transactionId) {"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 384,
            "text": "        return { ...sim, status: 'inactive', deactivationDate: new Date().toISOString() };"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 385,
            "text": "      }"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 386,
            "text": "      return sim;"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 387,
            "text": "    });"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 388
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 389,
            "text": "    await db.collection('users').doc(uid).update({"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 390,
            "text": "      registeredSims: updatedSims,"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 391,
            "text": "      updatedAt: new Date()"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 392,
            "text": "    });"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 393
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 394,
            "text": "    // Update SIM document status"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 395,
            "text": "    const simSnapshot = await db.collection('sims')"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 396,
            "text": "      .where('transactionId', '==', transactionId)"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 397,
            "text": "      .limit(1)"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 398,
            "text": "      .get();"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 399
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 400,
            "text": "    if (!simSnapshot.empty) {"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 401,
            "text": "      await db.collection('sims').doc(simSnapshot.docs[0].id).update({"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 402,
            "text": "        status: 'inactive',"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 403,
            "text": "        deactivationDate: new Date(),"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 404,
            "text": "        updatedAt: new Date()"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 405,
            "text": "      });"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 406
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 407,
            "text": "      // Log to blockchain"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 408,
            "text": "      try {"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 409,
            "text": "        await submitToBlockchain({"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 410,
            "text": "          action: 'deactivateSim',"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 411,
            "text": "          cnic: user.cnic,"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 412,
            "text": "          mobileNumber: simToDeactivate.mobileNumber,"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 413,
            "text": "          transactionId,"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 414,
            "text": "          timestamp: new Date().toISOString()"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 415,
            "text": "        });"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 416,
            "text": "      } catch (fabricError) {"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 417,
            "text": "        console.error('Fabric logging error:', fabricError);"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 418,
            "text": "      }"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 419,
            "text": "    }"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 420
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 421,
            "text": "    // Audit log"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 422,
            "text": "    await auditLog({"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 423,
            "text": "      userId: uid,"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 424,
            "text": "      action: 'SIM_DEACTIVATION',"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 425,
            "text": "      details: {"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 426,
            "text": "        transactionId,"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 427,
            "text": "        mobileNumber: simToDeactivate.mobileNumber,"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 428,
            "text": "        timestamp: new Date().toISOString()"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 429,
            "text": "      },"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 430,
            "text": "      ipAddress: req.ip,"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 431,
            "text": "      userAgent: req.get('user-agent')"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 432,
            "text": "    });"

          },

          {

            "text": "      message: 'SIM deactivated successfully'"
          },

            "lineNumber": 438
          },
          {

          }

    {
      "codeBlock": {
        "detailedLines": [
          {
            "lineNumber": 1,
            "text": "# DIMS-SR Backend API"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 2
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 3,
            "text": "Digital Identity Management System - SIM Registration backend built with Node.js, Express, PostgreSQL, and Hyperledger Fabric."
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 4
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 5,
            "text": "## Features"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 6
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 7,
            "text": "- **Secure Authentication**: JWT-based authentication with refresh tokens"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 8,
            "text": "- **Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)**: TOTP-based with authenticator apps"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 9,
            "text": "- **Hyperledger Fabric Integration**: Blockchain-based SIM registration and validation"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 10,
            "text": "- **TLS/SSL Encryption**: End-to-end encrypted communication via HTTPS"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 11,
            "text": "- **Session Management**: Secure HTTP-only cookies with CSRF protection"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 12,
            "text": "- **Rate Limiting**: Protection against brute force attacks"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 13,
            "text": "- **Input Validation**: Comprehensive validation and sanitization"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 14,
            "text": "- **Audit Logging**: Complete event logging for compliance"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 15,
            "text": "- **Security Headers**: Helmet.js for HTTP security headers"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 16,
            "text": "- **SQL Injection Prevention**: Parameterized queries"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 17,
            "text": "- **Session Hijacking Prevention**: IP and User-Agent verification"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 18
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 19,
            "text": "## Prerequisites"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 20
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 21,
            "text": "- Node.js 16+ and npm"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 22,
            "text": "- PostgreSQL 12+"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 23,
            "text": "- Hyperledger Fabric 2.2+"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 24,
            "text": "- OpenSSL for certificate generation"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 25
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 26,
            "text": "## Installation"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 27
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 28,
            "text": "1. **Clone the repository**"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 29,
            "text": "   &#96;&#96;&#96;bash"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 30,
            "text": "   cd backend"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 31,
            "text": "   &#96;&#96;&#96;"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 32
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 33,
            "text": "2. **Install dependencies**"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 34,
            "text": "   &#96;&#96;&#96;bash"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 35,
            "text": "   npm install"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 36,
            "text": "   &#96;&#96;&#96;"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 37
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 38,
            "text": "3. **Setup environment variables**"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 39,
            "text": "   &#96;&#96;&#96;bash"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 40,
            "text": "   cp .env.example .env"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 41,
            "text": "   # Edit .env with your configuration"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 42,
            "text": "   &#96;&#96;&#96;"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 43
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 44,
            "text": "4. **Generate SSL/TLS certificates** (for development)"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 45,
            "text": "   &#96;&#96;&#96;bash"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 46,
            "text": "   chmod +x scripts/generate-certs.sh"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 47,
            "text": "   ./scripts/generate-certs.sh"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 48,
            "text": "   &#96;&#96;&#96;"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 49
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 50,
            "text": "5. **Setup PostgreSQL database**"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 51,
            "text": "   &#96;&#96;&#96;bash"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 52,
            "text": "   chmod +x scripts/setup-db.sh"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 53,
            "text": "   ./scripts/setup-db.sh"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 54,
            "text": "   &#96;&#96;&#96;"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 55
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 56,
            "text": "6. **Setup Hyperledger Fabric network**"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 57,
            "text": "   &#96;&#96;&#96;bash"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 58,
            "text": "   docker-compose -f ../docker-compose-fabric.yml up -d"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 59,
            "text": "   &#96;&#96;&#96;"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 60
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 61,
            "text": "7. **Deploy chaincode**"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 62,
            "text": "   &#96;&#96;&#96;bash"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 63,
            "text": "   # Instructions in chaincode directory"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 64,
            "text": "   cd ../chaincode"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 65,
            "text": "   &#96;&#96;&#96;"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 66
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 67,
            "text": "## Running the Server"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 68
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 69,
            "text": "### Development"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 70,
            "text": "&#96;&#96;&#96;bash"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 71,
            "text": "npm run dev"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 72,
            "text": "&#96;&#96;&#96;"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 73,
            "text": "Server runs on https://localhost:3001"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 74
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 75,
            "text": "### Production"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 76,
            "text": "&#96;&#96;&#96;bash"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 77,
            "text": "NODE_ENV=production npm start"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 78,
            "text": "&#96;&#96;&#96;"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 79,
            "text": "Ensure proper environment variables are set in production."
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 80
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 81,
            "text": "## API Endpoints"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 82
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 83,
            "text": "### Authentication"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 84,
            "text": "- &#96;POST /api/auth/signup&#96; - Create new account"
          }
        ],
        "range": {
          "endPosition": {
            "line": 84
          },
          "startPosition": {}
        },
        "relativeWorkspacePath": "./backend/README.md",
        "signatures": {}
      },
      "score": 0.18456420302391052
    },

      "codeBlock": {

          {
            "lineNumber": 56,
            "text": "  # CouchDB for Org1"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 57,
            "text": "  couchdb0:"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 58,
            "text": "    image: couchdb:3.1"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 59,
            "text": "    environment:"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 60,
            "text": "      COUCHDB_USER: admin"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 61,
            "text": "      COUCHDB_PASSWORD: password"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 62,
            "text": "    ports:"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 63,
            "text": "      - \"5984:5984\""
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 64,
            "text": "    networks:"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 65,
            "text": "      - fabric-network"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 66
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 67,
            "text": "  # CLI for managing network"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 68,
            "text": "  cli:"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 69,
            "text": "    image: hyperledger/fabric-tools:latest"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 70,
            "text": "    tty: true"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 71,
            "text": "    environment:"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 72,
            "text": "      GOPATH: /opt/gopath"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 73,
            "text": "      FABRIC_CFG_PATH: /etc/hyperledger/fabric"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 74,
            "text": "      CORE_PEER_ID: cli"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 75,
            "text": "      CORE_PEER_ADDRESS: peer0.org1.dims-sr.local:7051"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 76,
            "text": "      CORE_PEER_LOCALMSPID: Org1MSP"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 77,
            "text": "      CORE_PEER_MSPCONFIGPATH: /etc/hyperledger/msp/users/Admin@org1.dims-sr.local/msp"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 78,
            "text": "    volumes:"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 79,
            "text": "      - ./fabric-network/peer0.org1/msp:/etc/hyperledger/msp/peer"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 80,
            "text": "      - ./chaincode:/opt/gopath/src/github.com/dims-sr/chaincode"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 81,
            "text": "      - ./fabric-network/config:/etc/hyperledger/fabric"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 82,
            "text": "    networks:"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 83,
            "text": "      - fabric-network"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 84,
            "text": "    depends_on:"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 85,
            "text": "      - orderer.dims-sr.local"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 86,
            "text": "      - peer0.org1.dims-sr.local"
          }
        ],

    {
      "codeBlock": {
        "detailedLines": [
          {
            "lineNumber": 1,
            "text": "# DIMS-SR (Digital Identity Management System - SIM Registration)"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 2
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 3,
            "text": "A comprehensive SIM registration system with biometric verification, built with Next.js frontend and Node.js/Express backend, designed for Vercel deployment."
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 4
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 5,
            "text": "## Features"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 6
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 7,
            "text": "- **User Authentication**: Secure login/signup with JWT tokens"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 8,
            "text": "- **Biometric Verification**: Fingerprint capture and NADRA verification"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 9,
            "text": "- **SIM Registration**: Complete SIM registration workflow"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 10,
            "text": "- **Order Tracking**: Track SIM registration orders"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 11,
            "text": "- **Multi-Factor Authentication**: Enhanced security with MFA"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 12,
            "text": "- **Hyperledger Fabric Integration**: Blockchain-based SIM management"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 13,
            "text": "- **Firebase Integration**: Real-time database and authentication"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 14
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 15,
            "text": "## Tech Stack"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 16
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 17,
            "text": "- **Frontend**: Next.js 14, React, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 18,
            "text": "- **Backend**: Node.js, Express.js, Firebase Admin SDK"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 19,
            "text": "- **Database**: Firebase Firestore"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 20,
            "text": "- **Blockchain**: Hyperledger Fabric"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 21,
            "text": "- **Biometric**: NADRA API integration"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 22,
            "text": "- **Deployment**: Vercel (monorepo setup)"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 23
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 24,
            "text": "## Project Structure"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 25
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 26,
            "text": "&#96;&#96;&#96;"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 27,
            "text": "├── app/                    # Next.js app directory"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 28,
            "text": "├── backend/               # Express.js backend"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 29,
            "text": "│   ├── api/              # Vercel serverless functions"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 30,
            "text": "│   ├── routes/           # API routes"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 31,
            "text": "│   ├── middleware/       # Express middleware"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 32,
            "text": "│   └── config/           # Database and service configs"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 33,
            "text": "├── components/           # React components"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 34,
            "text": "├── lib/                  # Utility functions"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 35,
            "text": "├── public/               # Static assets"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 36,
            "text": "└── vercel.json          # Vercel configuration"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 37,
            "text": "&#96;&#96;&#96;"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 38
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 39,
            "text": "## Local Development"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 40
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 41,
            "text": "### Prerequisites"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 42
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 43,
            "text": "- Node.js 18+"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 44,
            "text": "- pnpm or npm"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 45,
            "text": "- Firebase project"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 46,
            "text": "- Hyperledger Fabric network (optional)"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 47
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 48,
            "text": "### Installation"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 49
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 50,
            "text": "1. Clone the repository"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 51,
            "text": "2. Install dependencies:"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 52,
            "text": "   &#96;&#96;&#96;bash"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 53,
            "text": "   pnpm install"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 54,
            "text": "   cd backend \u0026\u0026 pnpm install \u0026\u0026 cd .."
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 55,
            "text": "   &#96;&#96;&#96;"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 56
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 57,
            "text": "3. Set up environment variables:"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 58,
            "text": "   &#96;&#96;&#96;bash"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 59,
            "text": "   cp .env.example .env.local"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 60,
            "text": "   # Edit .env.local with your configuration"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 61,
            "text": "   &#96;&#96;&#96;"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 62
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 63,
            "text": "4. Start development servers:"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 64,
            "text": "   &#96;&#96;&#96;bash"
          },
          {

        "detailedLines": [
          {
            "lineNumber": 273,
            "text": "- Docker \u0026 Docker Compose"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 274,
            "text": "- Node.js Chaincode Runtime"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 275
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 276,
            "text": "---"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 277
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 278,
            "text": "## Deployment Instructions"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 279
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 280,
            "text": "### 1. Database Setup"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 281,
            "text": "&#96;&#96;&#96;bash"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 282,
            "text": "cd backend"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 283,
            "text": "chmod +x scripts/setup-db.sh"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 284,
            "text": "./scripts/setup-db.sh"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 285,
            "text": "&#96;&#96;&#96;"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 286
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 287,
            "text": "### 2. SSL/TLS Certificates (Development)"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 288,
            "text": "&#96;&#96;&#96;bash"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 289,
            "text": "chmod +x scripts/generate-certs.sh"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 290,
            "text": "./scripts/generate-certs.sh"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 291,
            "text": "&#96;&#96;&#96;"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 292
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 293,
            "text": "### 3. Hyperledger Fabric Network"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 294,
            "text": "&#96;&#96;&#96;bash"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 295,
            "text": "docker-compose -f docker-compose-fabric.yml up -d"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 296,
            "text": "&#96;&#96;&#96;"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 297
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 298,
            "text": "### 4. Backend API"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 299,
            "text": "&#96;&#96;&#96;bash"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 300,
            "text": "cd backend"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 301,
            "text": "npm install"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 302,
            "text": "npm start  # Production"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 303,
            "text": "npm run dev  # Development"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 304,
            "text": "&#96;&#96;&#96;"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 305
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 306,
            "text": "### 5. Frontend (Already built and running)"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 307,
            "text": "&#96;&#96;&#96;bash"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 308,
            "text": "npm run dev"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 309,
            "text": "&#96;&#96;&#96;"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 310
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 311,
            "text": "### 6. Mobile App"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 312,
            "text": "&#96;&#96;&#96;bash"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 313,
            "text": "cd mobile"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 314,
            "text": "npm install"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 315,
            "text": "npx expo start"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 316,
            "text": "&#96;&#96;&#96;"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 317
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 318,
            "text": "### 7. Desktop App"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 319,
            "text": "&#96;&#96;&#96;bash"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 320,
            "text": "cd desktop"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 321,
            "text": "npm install"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 322,
            "text": "npm run electron-dev   # Development"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 323,
            "text": "npm run build          # Production build"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 324,
            "text": "&#96;&#96;&#96;"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 325
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 326,
            "text": "---"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 327
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 328,
            "text": "## Environment Configuration"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 329
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 330,
            "text": "Create &#96;.env&#96; file in &#96;/backend&#96; with:"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 331
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 332,
            "text": "&#96;&#96;&#96;env"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 333,
            "text": "# Server"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 334,
            "text": "PORT=3001"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 335,
            "text": "NODE_ENV=production"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 336,
            "text": "API_URL=https://localhost:3001"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 337
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 338,
            "text": "# Database"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 339,
            "text": "DB_HOST=localhost"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 340,
            "text": "DB_PORT=5432"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 341,
            "text": "DB_USER=dims_user"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 342,
            "text": "DB_PASSWORD=\u003cstrong_password\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 343,
            "text": "DB_NAME=dims_sr_db"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 344
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 345,
            "text": "# JWT"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 346,
            "text": "JWT_SECRET=\u003cmin_32_chars\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 347,
            "text": "JWT_EXPIRY=7d"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 348,
            "text": "REFRESH_TOKEN_SECRET=\u003cmin_32_chars\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 349,
            "text": "REFRESH_TOKEN_EXPIRY=30d"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 350
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 351,
            "text": "# Sessions"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 352,
            "text": "SESSION_SECRET=\u003cmin_32_chars\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 353,
            "text": "SESSION_EXPIRY=24h"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 354,
            "text": "SECURE_COOKIES=true"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 355
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 356,
            "text": "# Hyperledger Fabric"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 357,
            "text": "FABRIC_CA_URL=http://localhost:7054"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 358,
            "text": "FABRIC_CHANNEL_NAME=dims-sr-channel"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 359,
            "text": "FABRIC_CHAINCODE_NAME=sim-registry"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 360
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 361,
            "text": "# Security"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 362,
            "text": "CORS_ORIGIN=https://localhost:3000"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 363,
            "text": "RATE_LIMIT_WINDOW_MS=900000"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 364,
            "text": "RATE_LIMIT_MAX_REQUESTS=100"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 365
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 366,
            "text": "# TLS/SSL"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 367,
            "text": "SSL_CERT_PATH=./certs/server.crt"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 368,
            "text": "SSL_KEY_PATH=./certs/server.key"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 369,
            "text": "&#96;&#96;&#96;"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 370
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 371,
            "text": "---"

The chaincode's `registerSIM` function accepts many parameters, while the `firebase-sim-registration` service calls `submitToBlockchain` with a different structure. Add a new chaincode function `recordRegistration` that stores audit events as immutable ledger entries using JSON strings. This function should use the transaction ID as a key to ensure immutability and align with the current architecture where Firestore remains the source of truth while Fabric serves as an immutable audit log. The implementation should store the JSON payload under a key like `AUDIT_<txId>`.

- Use `ctx.stub.getTxID()` to generate unique transaction ID
- Store payload as JSON string in ledger with key format `AUDIT_<txId>`
- Ensure the entry is immutable and cannot be overwritten
- Maintain compatibility with existing app flow where Firestore is primary source of truth The existing `registerSIM` function duplicates logic with Firestore, so a new `recordAudit` function is added to the `sim-registry` chaincode. This function stores audit events as immutable ledger entries using the transaction ID as a key. It accepts an action and a JSON string payload, records the timestamp, and stores the data under a key formatted as `AUDIT_<txId>`. The Node.js `submitToBlockchain` function then calls `recordAudit` with the appropriate action and JSON string. A chaincode redeploy is required to add the new function and update the fabric-gateway.

The fabric-gateway implementation uses `@hyperledger/fabric-gateway` (v1.x) and requires:
- Connection configuration (connection.json)
- TLS certificate paths from environment variables
- User private key for signing transactions

The backend already includes `fabric-network` in its package.json, and the root package has `"@hyperledger/fabric-gateway": "1.10.1"`. The backend package.json will be updated to include fabric-gateway, and the implementation will follow the Hyperledger fabric-gateway node example for submitting transactions. The backend already includes `fabric-network` version 2.2, which will be used with the classic Gateway API and connection profile. The implementation will follow Hyperledgers fabric-network documentation for version 2.2, using `fabric-network` instead of `fabric-gateway` for transaction submission. The connection profile path is sourced from the environment variable `FABRIC_CCP_PATH`, and the connection details are loaded from a local file. A wallet is created using the `Wallets` module to manage identities for signing transactions. The `submitToBlockchain` function will use these components to connect to the network and submit transactions. No changes are needed for the `recordAudit` chaincode function or the `submitToBlockchain` implementation since they are compatible with `fabric-network` v2.2. The `recordAudit` chaincode function will accept string arguments only, with the JSON payload passed as a single string argument. This aligns with Fabric's requirement for string-based arguments in chaincode functions.

The `submitToBlockchain` function now calls `recordAudit` with the JSON string payload, ensuring audit events are stored immutably on the ledger using the transaction ID as the key.

No further changes are needed for the chaincode or backend implementation since they are already compatible with the existing setup. I'll add the following chaincode function to record audit events:

- Function name: `recordAudit`
- Accepts: JSON string payload as single argument
- Generates: Transaction ID using `ctx.stub.getTxID()`
- Stores: JSON record under key format `AUDIT_<txId>` with committed timestamp
- Emits: Event with txId and action
- Returns: Key and txId as JSON string

If Fabric is not configured, `submitToBlockchain` should skip blockchain submission and log a warning instead of throwing an error. If **FABRIC_ENABLED=false**, log a warning and skip submission; in production, if **FABRIC_ENABLED=true**, require Fabric to be operational and fail SIM registration on connection failure.  
If **NODE_ENV=production** and required environment variables are missing, throw an error during module load or return an error from `submitToBlockchain`.  

Policy:
- When **FABRIC_ENABLED=true** (default in production), submit to chaincode; on failure, propagate error to prevent SIM registration. If `FABRIC_REQUIRED=true` in production, missing Fabric configuration should throw an error at startup or during submission to prevent SIM registration. Otherwise, if `FABRIC_ENABLED` is not set to 'false', the system attempts to connect; it skips submission and logs a warning only when `FABRIC_REQUIRED` is not set to 'true'. The backend uses `FABRIC_GATEWAY_CONFIG_PATH`, `FABRIC_WALLET_PATH`, and `FABRIC_USER_IDENTITY` for connection settings. If `FABRIC_REQUIRED=true` and Fabric is not configured, `submitToBlockchain` should now throw an error instead of catching and continuing, to ensure proper handling in production.

A new configuration file `fabric-gateway.js` will be added to handle gateway connections and lazy initialization, including a `recordAudit` transaction call. <think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The nadra-mock-server functions as a hybrid system that incorporates both scraping and MOCK fallback mechanisms. For production, `nadra-service.js` should call `NADRA_API_BASE_URL` pointing to either the running `nadra-mock-server` (external process) at `http://localhost:4000` or the actual NADRA API URL from the government.

- The standalone server can be labeled **nadra-adapter**.
- Main backend never embeds mock data; it only calls HTTP.
- The separate server acts as a "NADRA adapter" service.
- In production, replace with real NADRA API URL from government. The nadra-mock-server is updated to be labeled as "NADRA adapter service (optional) - scraping + cache," and duplicate MOCK handling is removed from nadra.js routes by routing to nadra-service HTTP.

- nadra-service.js now uses axios for HTTP requests.
- It retrieves the base URL from environment variables, trimming trailing slashes.
- If base URL is missing, it returns null.
- The verifyUserWithNadra function makes an HTTP request to the NADRA API using the configured base URL. If the NADRA API base URL is not configured, the system returns a failure response indicating that NADRA integration is missing. Otherwise, it sends a POST request to the NADRA API endpoint `/verify-user` with user details such as CNIC, name, father's name, and date of birth. The response is parsed to determine if the user is verified, and any errors during the request are thrown as exceptions.

The same verification logic applies to fingerprint checks.

In production, Firebase authentication signup relies on `verifyUserWithNadra`; if the user is not verified, the signup process fails. If NADRA is not configured, verification defaults to false, causing signup to fail; documentation should state that the adapter must be run or the URL set.

- In development, behavior remains the same unless `NADRA_ALLOW_BYPASS=true` is used for dev-only bypass.
- For FABRIC: implement `fabric-gateway` with `fabric-network`, using a connection profile template (`fabric/connection-profile.example.json`) aligned with test-network Org1.
- Wallet implementation uses `Wallets.newFileSystemWallet` from Fabric samples; certificate export from the test network is required. The peer organization admin typically uses an enrolled identity like `appUser` from fabric-ca or the Org1 admin identity for backend operations  **production requires enrolled identities**.

For simplicity with Docker secrets, use **InMemoryWallet** with certificate and key paths from environment variables `FABRIC_CLIENT_CERT_PATH` and `FABRIC_CLIENT_KEY_PATH`.

Following the fabric-network 2.2 pattern:
- Use `Wallets.newInMemoryWallet()` for wallet creation.
- Read credentials from `FABRIC_CREDENTIALS_DIR`.
- Load certificate and private key files using `fs.readFileSync`. The code reads the certificate and private key from the specified paths and creates an X.509 identity, which is then stored in the wallet under a label (defaulting to 'appUser').

- Uses `fs.readFileSync` to load certificate and private key files.
- Constructs an identity object with the certificate and key.
- Stores the identity in the wallet with the MSP ID and type set to 'X.509'.
- Returns the configured wallet.

**Lazy singleton Gateway**: connects once and reuses the connection with error recovery mechanisms.

**Chaincode `recordAudit`**: 
```javascript
``` The `recordAudit` function generates a unique key using the transaction ID and stores the payload JSON string directly. The `submitToBlockchain` function in Node.js submits the audit data to the chaincode using `recordAudit` with the data serialized as JSON.

- Key generation uses `txId` for uniqueness.
- Payload is stored as a JSON string.
- Chaincode interaction occurs via `contract.submitTransaction`. **Chaincode name mismatch**: The package name is `sim-registry`, but the contract class name is `SIMRegistryContract`. In Fabric 2.x, the chaincode label is `simregistry` and the contract ID is `SIMRegistryContract`. The default contract name in `fabric-contract-api` matches the class name `SIMRegistryContract`.

For `fabric-shim`, `module.exports` includes `contracts: [SIMRegistryContract]`, so the default contract name is derived from the class name.

When invoking chaincode, `getContract` requires the **chaincode name** as installed on the peer, and `submitTransaction` targets the first contract or specifies the contract name explicitly.

Example:
```javascript
const contract = await network.getContract('simregistry');
```</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
I'll create a helper function `submitFabricAudit(payload)` that throws an error when FABRIC_REQUIRED is enabled and the submission fails. Refactor fabric-gateway to export:
- `submitToBlockchain(data)` - real implementation

Set `FABRIC_ENABLED` to `true` | `false` - if false, skip fabric entirely (not recommended for prod). 
If `FABRIC_ENABLED=true` (default), require connection and submit.
Use `FABRIC_REQUIRED` = `true` | `false` - if false, skip fabric entirely (not recommended for prod).
If `FABRIC_REQUIRED=true` (default), require connection and submit.
When `FABRIC_REQUIRED=true`, throw error on submission failure.
When `FABRIC_REQUIRED=false`, allow submission to proceed without fabric. If `FABRIC_ENABLED=false` is set explicitly, skip fabric entirely (useful for labs without fabric). For production, `FABRIC_ENABLED=true` must be set.  
If `FABRIC_ENABLED` is missing, throw a clear error instead of silently mocking.  
If fabric submission fails, throw the error so the caller can handle it appropriately.  
Update `firebase-sim-registration` to await `submitToBlockchain` without swallowing errors when fabric is required.  

Change try/catch block to:
```javascript
const fabricRequired = process.env.FABRIC_REQUIRED !== 'false';
try {
  await submitToBlockchain(...);
} catch (e) {
``` If `fabricRequired` is true, the system should rollback Firestore changes, but this would require a larger refactor to change transaction orderwriting to Firestore first, then submitting to Fabric. A safer approach for production avoids rewriting the entire flow:

- Write to Firestore first (current behavior)
- Submit to Fabric afterward
- If Fabric fails and `FABRIC_REQUIRED` is true, enqueue a retry in Firestores `fabricPendingWrites` collection with a background job

This ensures data consistency without major architectural changes. The simpler production approach keeps Firestore writes before Fabric submission. However, reordering to validate first, then submitToBlockchain, then create the SIM document ensures that Fabric submission happens before Firestore commit. If Fabric fails, no data is written  which is preferred behavior. This avoids complex rollback logic while maintaining data integrity.</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Legacy user routes `setup-mfa/confirm-mfa` could be utilized by mobile devices, which use `/user/setup-mfa` against the API base, potentially involving a different backend. I'll fix confirm-mfa to use real speakeasy with Firestore like firebase-auth, and setup-mfa to read user from firestore and return real secretessentially duplicating the firebase-auth MFA functionality, which could then be deprecated.

For minimal disruption:
- Fix **confirm-mfa** in user.js to verify with speakeasy against user's mfaSecret from Firestore
- **setup-mfa** already generates mock secret and should generate speakeasy secret, saving to temp collection or updating user doc as pendingtoo complex - Remove setup-mfa and confirm-mfa from user router exports if mobile depends on `/api/user/setup-mfa`
- Implement setup-mfa in user.js using a delegate pattern: fetch user from Firestore, generate speakeasy secret, and save to user document as pending
- Replace hardcoded secret in setup-mfa with speakeasy.generateSecret and update Firestore user mfaSecret field
- Similar approach to signup flow for consistency confirm-mfa should use speakeasy.totp.verify with the user's mfaSecret retrieved from Firestore, aligning legacy mobile paths with actual cryptographic verification.

Implementation fabric-gateway.js:

- Use dynamic import or require for fabric-network to handle optional dependency
- Initialize Gateway instance only once using singleton pattern
- Load wallet and connection profile from filesystem
- Return gateway instance for Fabric network interaction

The implementation requires handling cases where Fabric may not be installed or configured, ensuring graceful fallback or error handling when fabric-network is unavailable. If the Fabric connection profile path is missing or invalid, an error is thrown. Otherwise, the connection profile is loaded from the file system, and a wallet is built. A Gateway instance is created and connected using the connection profile, wallet, and identity configuration. The gateway instance is stored and returned for use in interacting with the Fabric network. To ensure serverless compatibility, the gateway should be disconnected after each request to avoid issues with Vercel cold starts. A new gateway instance must be created per request rather than maintaining a persistent connection. This involves implementing a disconnect mechanism after each operation and using a factory pattern to manage gateway lifecycle per request.

- Disconnect gateway after each submit for serverless environments
- Create new gateway instance per request instead of reusing connections
- Implement proper cleanup and disposal of gateway resources
- Use factory pattern with gateway lifecycle management per request The `submitToBlockchain` function now properly disconnects the gateway after each operation and uses a factory pattern for serverless compatibility. It also handles Fabric disabled scenarios by skipping execution when `FABRIC_ENABLED=false`, returning `{ skipped: true }` instead of throwing. In production, `FABRIC_ENABLED` defaults to true, so missing or invalid Fabric configuration throws an error instead of silently skipping.

- When `FABRIC_ENABLED === 'false'`, Fabric operations are skipped explicitly
- Otherwise, Fabric is executed; missing configuration throws an error
- `firebase-sim-registration` no longer swallows errors from `submitToBlockchain` and instead returns 503 on failure after compensation
- Production defaults to `FABRIC_ENABLED=true` to prevent silent failures</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Fix getUserFromNadra - axios error might have err. - Use `err` from the catch block for the response.
- The client request uses `:cnic` in the path, but it's okay since it's properly encoded.
- Firebase auth signup calls `verifyUserWithNadra`, and if NADRA isn't configured, it returns `verified: false`, causing the user to see "Identity verification failed".
- For firebase-sim-registration, when NADRA returns `verified: false`, users get a 403 error and must configure NADRA.
- Keep the catch logic for handling "service unavailable" errors from NADRA. When `verifyFingerprintWithNadra` returns "not configured," the code currently checks `verified` and fails, which is correct. However, when NADRA throws an error, the code was previously skipping this case. The change ensures that if `images.length > 0` and NADRA throws an error, a 503 status is returned during registration.

In `user.js`, the order tracking now returns a 404 for empty cases instead of an empty response.

For multi-factor authentication (MFA), the `setup-mfa` and `confirm-mfa` functions in `user.js` need to be updated to use Firestore and speakeasy. The `setup` function may require an auth import for the user object.</details></think>

Register flow issue: if fabric fails after we've... We moved fabric before Firestore, which is good. If fabric succeeds but Firestore fails, the ledger has an entry without Firestoreacceptable for audit trail.

If fabric is disabled, submit returns skipped without throwing. submitToBlockchain doesn't throw when disabledgood. When fabric is disabled, fabric-gateway returns {skipped} without throwingalso good.

_**Agent (default)**_

Here is what was implemented and what you still must do on your infrastructure.

## What was wrong before

- **`fabric-gateway.js`** was a hard-coded mock (fake transaction IDs).
- **NADRA** used an **in-process mock citizen table** in `nadra-service.js` and `nadra.js`.
- **Track order** returned a **fake order** when nothing was in Firestore.
- **SIM registration** used a **fabricated multi-step timeline** and **ignored Fabric failures**.
- **Fingerprint / NADRA errors** were sometimes **ignored** so flows continued without real verification.
- **Legacy `sim-registration.js`** exposed **mock** GET handlers.
- **`/api/user/setup-mfa` / `confirm-mfa`** used **fake secrets / any 6 digits**.
- **MFA setup UI** showed a **random manual key** instead of the server secret.

## What is implemented now

### Hyperledger Fabric

- **Chaincode** (`chaincode/sim-registry/index.js`): new **`recordAudit(payloadJson)`** — writes one state key per tx (`AUDIT_<txId>`) and emits `AuditRecorded`.
- **`backend/config/fabric-gateway.js`**: real **`fabric-network`** `Gateway` + `submitTransaction('recordAudit', JSON.stringify(data))`, with disconnect after each call.  
  - **`FABRIC_ENABLED=false`** → skip ledger (for local UI only).  
  - Otherwise **missing profile / wallet → throws** (no mock success).
- **SIM register / deactivate** (Firebase routes): **ledger write runs before Firestore**; on failure returns **503** (when Fabric is not disabled). **Legacy deactivate** path fixed the same way and **SIM lookup by document id** (no bogus `id` field query).
- **Orders**: **only a real `confirmed` timeline entry** at creation (no fake future shipped/delivered timestamps).
- **Docs**: `fabric/README.md`, `fabric/connection-profile.example.json`, updated **`backend/.env.example`**.  
- **`docker-compose-fabric.yml`**: comment that it is a **skeleton**; real path is **fabric-samples/test-network** (documented).

You must **package, install, approve, and commit** the updated chaincode on your channel (same name as `FABRIC_CHAINCODE_NAME`, default **`simregistry`**).

### NADRA

- **`nadra-service.js`** only calls **`NADRA_API_BASE_URL`** (`/verify-user`, `/verify-fingerprint`, `/citizen/:cnic`). **No embedded citizen DB** in the API.
- **`nadra.js`** routes proxy to that service; **503** if not configured.
- **Signup** returns **503** with a clear message if NADRA is “not configured”.
- The **standalone** `nadra-mock-server.js` is only an **optional adapter process** (rename in scripts to `nadra-adapter`). It may still cache/scrape internally; that is **outside** the main API.

### Other production cleanups

- **Track order**: **404** if no Firestore order (no mock payload).
- **User MFA legacy routes**: **real `speakeasy` secrets** on setup; **real TOTP check** on confirm + Firestore updates + audit log.
- **`sim-registration.js`**: mock GETs replaced with **501** and pointers to Firebase-backed routes.
- **`mfa-setup.tsx`**: manual key from **`manualEntry`** in `/api/auth/mfa/setup` response.
- **`backend/package.json`**: explicit **`firebase-admin`**; script **`nadra-adapter`**.

---

## What you must configure to run “for real”

1. **Fabric** (see `fabric/README.md`): run a real network (recommended: **fabric-samples/test-network**), deploy chaincode with **`recordAudit`**, then set:
   - `FABRIC_CONNECTION_PROFILE_PATH`
   - `FABRIC_USER_CREDENTIALS_DIR` **or** `FABRIC_WALLET_PATH`
   - `FABRIC_CHANNEL_NAME`, `FABRIC_CHAINCODE_NAME`, `FABRIC_DISCOVERY_AS_LOCALHOST` as appropriate  
   For laptops without a network: **`FABRIC_ENABLED=false`** (not for production).

2. **NADRA**: set **`NADRA_API_BASE_URL`** to your adapter (e.g. `http://127.0.0.1:4000` if you run `pnpm run nadra-adapter` in `backend/`).

3. **Redeploy** backend + reinstall chaincode on peers after pulling these changes.

---

## Honest limit on “deploy Fabric from here”

Spinning up **production** Fabric (CAs, TLS, ordering, RAFT/BFT, peer HA, CouchDB/LevelDB, backups, monitoring) is **infrastructure work on your cloud/VPC**, not something this repo can finish by itself. This change set gives you **production-style application integration** (real SDK, no mock gateway, strict errors) and a **documented path** using the official **test-network** workflow. For a thesis/demo, test-network + a small VM is usually enough; for national-scale production you would use a managed offering or a hardened multi-org design with operations runbooks.

If you want a next step in-repo, we can add a **`fabric/scripts/`** lifecycle script tailored to your exact `test-network` paths and chaincode version once you confirm Fabric version (2.4 / 2.5) and channel/chaincode names you deployed.

---

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The user needs to keep mock NADRA functionality active, including mock users and fingerprint verification, to ensure the project runs properly while they set up the real NADRA HTTP adapter later. The user wants to update `nadra-service.js` to conditionally use either HTTP requests or mock data based on environment variables. When `NADRA_API_BASE_URL` is set, it should use HTTP; otherwise, it should fall back to mock data and fingerprint verification.

To maintain compatibility, the embedded mock should be restored in `nadra-service.js` when `NADRA_API_BASE_URL` is not configured, using the same mock data as before.

Key points:
- Check if `NADRA_API_BASE_URL` is set
- If set, use HTTP requests
- If not set, use embedded mock data and mock fingerprint verification
- Preserve existing mock data structure from previous implementation - **nadra-service.js**: if `getBaseUrl()` returns null, use inline mock data; otherwise, use HTTP requests.
- **nadra.js routes**: remain HTTP-only, but `nadra-service.js` will fall back to mock internally when the base URL is not configured.
- Restore `MOCK_NADRA_DATA`, `verifyUserWithNadra`, `verifyFingerprintWithNadra`, and `getUserFromNadra` logic from the original implementation or nadra-mock-server for consistency. When `getBaseUrl()` returns null, `nadra-service.js` should use mock helpers instead of returning 503 errors, ensuring that routes like `/api/nadra/verify-user` function properly without requiring an external adapter.

- If `NADRA_API_BASE_URL` is not set, `nadra-service.js` will use embedded mock data and logic.
- Routes in `nadra.js` will proxy to the HTTP adapter when `getBaseUrl()` is available; otherwise, theyll call the service's mock functions directly.
- This approach keeps mock functionality consistent and avoids breaking endpoints when the real adapter isn't configured.</details></think>

Here is what was restored and how it behaves.

### Behavior

1. **`backend/utils/nadra-service.js`**
   - **`NADRA_API_BASE_URL` / `NADRA_ADAPTER_URL` not set** (typical FYP / local): uses the **same three mock CNICs** as before (`34203-6348972-7`, `42301-7331552-4`, `42101-1234567-8`) with name / father / DOB checks, and **mock fingerprint** success (any non-empty images → verified, ~95% match, deterministic hash).
   - **URL set**: unchanged — still calls the real HTTP adapter (`/verify-user`, `/verify-fingerprint`, `/citizen/...`).

2. **`backend/routes/nadra.js`**
   - Removed the **503 “adapter not configured”** gate so `/api/nadra/*` always goes through the service (mock or HTTP).

3. **`backend/.env.example`**
   - Documents that **`NADRA_API_BASE_URL` is optional**; mock is the default when it is omitted.

4. **`fabric/README.md`**
   - NADRA section updated to describe the **mock default** and optional adapter.

Signup and SIM registration again work **without** running the standalone NADRA server, as long as you use the mock CNICs (and matching names where required). When you later set `NADRA_API_BASE_URL`, the same code paths switch to the live adapter only.

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
On registration flows, submitToBlockchain is invoked. When a user registers a SIM, it invokes submitToBlockchain. If FABRIC_ENABLED is unset, the system attempts to load Fabric network and may crash due to missing CryptoSuite.

For Vercel deployments, we need one of these options:
- Set FABRIC_ENABLED=false in Vercel environment variables (documented)
- Default fabricExplicitlyDisabled() to true when serverless signals like VERCEL or AWS_LAMBDA_FUNCTION_NAME are detected, unless explicitly enabled

Option 2 provides better developer experience for Vercel deployments since Fabric rarely runs there. The implementation would check for serverless environment variables and disable Fabric automatically unless explicitly enabled. When `FABRIC_ENABLED` is unset and running on Vercel, Fabric is automatically disabled unless a connection profile path is provided. This simplifies the logic by treating unset values as disabled in serverless environments. When `FABRIC_ENABLED` is unset, Fabric is disabled by default on Vercel deployments unless explicitly set to `true`. For local development without Vercel, Fabric remains enabled by default to maintain previous behavior, but will throw an error if no connection profile is found. This approach ensures that Fabric is only active when explicitly intended, preventing crashes in serverless environments while preserving existing behavior for local development.

- On Vercel, Fabric is disabled unless `FABRIC_ENABLED=true`
- Locally, Fabric stays enabled unless `FABRIC_ENABLED=false`
- Unset values default to enabled in local environments but disabled in Vercel
- This prevents crashes when no connection profile exists in serverless environments When `FABRIC_ENABLED` is unset, the system defaults to disabling Fabric in Vercel environments to prevent crashes, while still allowing users to explicitly enable it. For local development, Fabric remains enabled by default unless explicitly disabled. This change ensures smoother deployments on Vercel without requiring manual configuration.

- Unset `FABRIC_ENABLED` disables Fabric on Vercel by default
- Local environments keep Fabric enabled unless explicitly disabled
- Vercel automatically disables Fabric unless `FABRIC_ENABLED=true` is set
- This prevents errors from missing connection profiles in serverless environments On Vercel, unset `FABRIC_ENABLED` defaults to disabled; locally, it remains enabled. Remove the try/catch block from `loadFabricNetwork` and add `fabric-common` to dependencies. Update `vercel.json` environment variables only if necessary, as the default handles most cases. Test with `node -e "process.chdir('backend'); require('./config/fabric-gateway'); console.log('ok')"` from the repo root.</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

Here is what was going wrong and what we changed.

### Cause

1. **`fabric-gateway.js` imported `fabric-network` at the top level.** Loading `backend/api/index.js` pulled in SIM routes → `fabric-gateway` → `fabric-network` on every cold start, even when you never call Fabric.

2. **`fabric-common` loads `CryptoSuite_ECDSA_AES` via a dynamic `require()` string** (from `Utils.js`). **Vercel’s Node bundler** (`@vercel/node` / nft) often **does not copy** those files into `/var/task`, so at runtime you get **Cannot find module 'fabric-common/lib/impl/CryptoSuite_ECDSA_AES'**.

### Fixes (in repo)

1. **Lazy load** – `fabric-network` (and the crypto impl) is only loaded inside `loadFabricNetwork()`, which runs **after** it is decided that Fabric should run.

2. **Explicit `require('fabric-common/lib/impl/CryptoSuite_ECDSA_AES')`** inside `loadFabricNetwork()` so the tracer is more likely to include that file **when** you do enable Fabric on serverless.

3. **Vercel-friendly default** – if `FABRIC_ENABLED` is **unset** and **`VERCEL=1`**, Fabric is treated as off (same outcome as skipping the SDK), so SIM flows work without setting env vars. Use **`FABRIC_ENABLED=true`** when you intentionally want on-chain submits from Vercel (and you still need a real connection profile / wallet in that environment).

4. **`fabric-common` added as a direct dependency** in `backend/package.json` so `require('fabric-common/...')` from your app is valid under pnpm.

After deploy, you should see a log like  
`[Fabric] Vercel default (set FABRIC_ENABLED=true to submit on-chain) — ledger submit skipped`  
when the ledger is skipped on Vercel.

**Local behavior:** unchanged unless `VERCEL=1` is set; unset `FABRIC_ENABLED` still means “try Fabric” when `submitToBlockchain` runs.

If you ever enable Fabric on Vercel and tracing still drops files, the next step is Vercel **`includeFiles`** for that function; say if you want that wired in `vercel.json`.

---

_**User**_

Now take me step by step and lets setup hyperledger fabric for our chain

---

- Create a network configuration file (network.yaml)
- Generate cryptographic materials using configtxgen
- Start the network using docker-compose
- Deploy chaincode using peer CLI or SDK
- Test the deployed chaincode via transactions

This setup enables developers to build, deploy, and test smart contracts on Hyperledger Fabric networks.
```</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The chaincode utilizes fabric-contract-api and fabric-shim libraries, running as an external service or packaged Node.js application. The standard manual lifecycle involves using `deployCC` helper scripts. The typical process includes:

- Cloning fabric-samples and navigating to test-network directory
- Running `./network.sh up createChannel -c mychannel -ca` to start network and create channel
- Setting environment variables for peer communication
- Packaging Node chaincode using `peer lifecycle chaincode package` command with appropriate path, language, and label parameters

The chaincode is packaged as a tar.gz file for deployment on the network. The chaincode package command requires the path to point to the directory containing `package.json` and `index.js`. After packaging, install the chaincode on peer0.org1, then approve and commit it. Alternatively, use `network.sh deployCC` with the `-ccp` flag pointing to the chaincode directory. For example:

- `./network.sh deployCC -ccn basic -ccp ../asset-transfer-basic/chaincode-javascript -ccl javascript`

This approach simplifies deployment by handling packaging and installation automatically. The chaincode can be deployed using the command:
- `./network.sh deployCC -ccn simregistry -ccp /path/to/explora-app-ui/chaincode/sim-registry -ccl javascript -c mychannel`

If fabric-samples is not available, follow these steps:
- Install prerequisites including Docker Desktop and sufficient RAM
- Clone fabric-samples repository at the matching release version (e.g., release-2.5 or main)
- Pull required Docker images using fabric install script or manually
- Start the test network with CA
- Deploy the chaincode using `network.sh deployCC` with appropriate parameters The `deployCC` helper script uses flags like `-ccn` (chaincode name), `-ccp` (chaincode path), `-ccl` (chaincode language), `-ccv` (version), `-c` (channel), and `-cci` (initialization function). For chaincodes without initialization, `recordAudit` suffices.

For chaincode packaging, the Node contract must export its class via `module.exports = class` in fabric-shim, or use `getContract` API in a metadata file. The fabric-contract-api typically expects the contract class to be exported directly. The chaincode package for Hyperledger Node contracts must expose contracts properly for discovery. For fabric-contract-api v2, ensure package.json includes `"start": "fabric-chaincode-node start"` and that the contract class is correctly exported. A metadata file or package.json entry may be needed to map contract names to classes, such as:

- `"fabric": { "contracts": { "SIMRegistryContract": "SIMRegistryContract" } }`

The contract class must match the expected naming convention for proper discovery by fabric-chaincode-node. The chaincode package.json file should include a start script pointing to the correct entry point, such as `"start": "fabric-chaincode-node start"`, which allows fabric-chaincode-node to locate and load the contract. The index.js file must export the contract class properly for discovery by the fabric-chaincode-node runtime. For fabric-contract-api v2.x, contracts are typically exported using `module.exports` with the contract class name matching the expected naming convention. The metadata configuration in package.json maps contract names to their respective classes, ensuring proper discovery during deployment. The chaincode contract class must be properly exported for fabric-chaincode-node to discover and load it. For fabric-contract-api v2.x, the contract class should be exported using `module.exports.ContractName = ContractClass` format. Alternatively, a metadata configuration in package.json can map contract names to their respective classes. The fabric-chaincode-node runtime expects contracts to be exported correctly to enable proper discovery and loading during deployment. The sim-registry example exports a single class directly via `module.exports = SIMRegistryContract`, while other samples use `module.exports.ContractName = ContractClass` pattern.</details></think>
