{
  "markdown": "#!/usr/bin/env bash\n#\n# Copyright IBM Corp All Rights Reserved\n#\n# SPDX-License-Identifier: Apache-2.0\n#\n\n# This script brings up a Hyperledger Fabric network for testing smart contracts\n# and applications. The test network consists of two organizations with one\n# peer each, and a single node Raft ordering service. Users can also use this\n# script to create a channel deploy a chaincode on the channel\n#\n# prepending $PWD/../bin to PATH to ensure we are picking up the correct binaries\n# this may be commented out to resolve installed version of tools if desired\n#\n# However using PWD in the path has the side effect that location that\n# this script is run from is critical. To ease this, get the directory\n# this script is actually in and infer location from there. (putting first)\n\nROOTDIR=$(cd \"$(dirname \"$0\")\" \u0026\u0026 pwd)\nexport PATH=${ROOTDIR}/../bin:${PWD}/../bin:$PATH\nexport FABRIC_CFG_PATH=${PWD}/configtx\nexport VERBOSE=false\n\n# push to the required directory \u0026 set a trap to go back if needed\npushd ${ROOTDIR} \u003e /dev/null\ntrap \"popd \u003e /dev/null\" EXIT\n\n. scripts/utils.sh\n\n: ${CONTAINER_CLI:=\"docker\"}\nif command -v ${CONTAINER_CLI}-compose \u003e /dev/null 2\u003e\u00261; then\n : ${CONTAINER_CLI_COMPOSE:=\"${CONTAINER_CLI}-compose\"}\nelse\n : ${CONTAINER_CLI_COMPOSE:=\"${CONTAINER_CLI} compose\"}\nfi\ninfoln \"Using ${CONTAINER_CLI} and ${CONTAINER_CLI_COMPOSE}\"\n\n# Obtain CONTAINER_IDS and remove them\n# This function is called when you bring a network down\nfunction clearContainers() {\n infoln \"Removing remaining containers\"\n ${CONTAINER_CLI} rm -f $(${CONTAINER_CLI} ps -aq --filter label=service=hyperledger-fabric) 2\u003e/dev/null || true\n ${CONTAINER_CLI} rm -f $(${CONTAINER_CLI} ps -aq --filter name='dev-peer*') 2\u003e/dev/null || true\n ${CONTAINER_CLI} kill \"$(${CONTAINER_CLI} ps -q --filter name=ccaas)\" 2\u003e/dev/null || true\n}\n\n# Delete any images that were generated as a part of this setup\n# specifically the following images are often left behind:\n# This function is called when you bring the network down\nfunction removeUnwantedImages() {\n infoln \"Removing generated chaincode docker images\"\n ${CONTAINER_CLI} image rm -f $(${CONTAINER_CLI} images -aq --filter reference='dev-peer*') 2\u003e/dev/null || true\n}\n\n# Versions of fabric known not to work with the test network\nNONWORKING_VERSIONS=\"^1\\.0\\. ^1\\.1\\. ^1\\.2\\. ^1\\.3\\. ^1\\.4\\.\"\n\n# Do some basic sanity checking to make sure that the appropriate versions of fabric\n# binaries/images are available. In the future, additional checking for the presence\n# of go or other items could be added.\nfunction checkPrereqs() {\n ## Check if your have cloned the peer binaries and configuration files.\n peer version \u003e /dev/null 2\u003e\u00261\n\n if [[ $? -ne 0 || ! -d \"../config\" ]]; then\n errorln \"Peer binary and configuration files not found..\"\n errorln\n errorln \"Follow the instructions in the Fabric docs to install the Fabric Binaries:\"\n errorln \"https://hyperledger-fabric.readthedocs.io/en/latest/install.html\"\n exit 1\n fi\n # use the fabric peer container to see if the samples and binaries match your\n # docker images\n LOCAL_VERSION=$(peer version | sed -ne 's/^ Version: //p')\n DOCKER_IMAGE_VERSION=$(${CONTAINER_CLI} run --rm hyperledger/fabric-peer:latest peer version | sed -ne 's/^ Version: //p')\n\n infoln \"LOCAL_VERSION=$LOCAL_VERSION\"\n infoln \"DOCKER_IMAGE_VERSION=$DOCKER_IMAGE_VERSION\"\n\n if [ \"$LOCAL_VERSION\" != \"$DOCKER_IMAGE_VERSION\" ]; then\n warnln \"Local fabric binaries and docker images are out of sync. This may cause problems.\"\n fi\n\n for UNSUPPORTED_VERSION in $NONWORKING_VERSIONS; do\n infoln \"$LOCAL_VERSION\" | grep -q $UNSUPPORTED_VERSION\n if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then\n fatalln \"Local Fabric binary version of $LOCAL_VERSION does not match the versions supported by the test network.\"\n fi\n\n infoln \"$DOCKER_IMAGE_VERSION\" | grep -q $UNSUPPORTED_VERSION\n if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then\n fatalln \"Fabric Docker image version of $DOCKER_IMAGE_VERSION does not match the versions supported by the test network.\"\n fi\n done\n\n ## check for cfssl binaries\n if [ \"$CRYPTO\" == \"cfssl\" ]; then\n \n cfssl version \u003e /dev/null 2\u003e\u00261\n if [[ $? -ne 0 ]]; then\n errorln \"cfssl binary not found..\"\n errorln\n errorln \"Follow the instructions to install the cfssl and cfssljson binaries:\"\n errorln \"https://github.com/cloudflare/cfssl#installation\"\n exit 1\n fi\n fi\n\n ## Check for fabric-ca\n if [ \"$CRYPTO\" == \"Certificate Authorities\" ]; then\n\n fabric-ca-client version \u003e /dev/null 2\u003e\u00261\n if [[ $? -ne 0 ]]; then\n errorln \"fabric-ca-client binary not found..\"\n errorln\n errorln \"Follow the instructions in the Fabric docs to install the Fabric Binaries:\"\n errorln \"https://hyperledger-fabric.readthedocs.io/en/latest/install.html\"\n exit 1\n fi\n CA_LOCAL_VERSION=$(fabric-ca-client version | sed -ne 's/ Version: //p')\n CA_DOCKER_IMAGE_VERSION=$(${CONTAINER_CLI} run --rm hyperledger/fabric-ca:latest fabric-ca-client version | sed -ne 's/ Version: //p' | head -1)\n infoln \"CA_LOCAL_VERSION=$CA_LOCAL_VERSION\"\n infoln \"CA_DOCKER_IMAGE_VERSION=$CA_DOCKER_IMAGE_VERSION\"\n\n if [ \"$CA_LOCAL_VERSION\" != \"$CA_DOCKER_IMAGE_VERSION\" ]; then\n warnln \"Local fabric-ca binaries and docker images are out of sync. This may cause problems.\"\n fi\n fi\n}\n\n# Before you can bring up a network, each organization needs to generate the crypto\n# material that will define that organization on the network. Because Hyperledger\n# Fabric is a permissioned blockchain, each node and user on the network needs to\n# use certificates and keys to sign and verify its actions. In addition, each user\n# needs to belong to an organization that is recognized as a member of the network.\n# You can use the Cryptogen tool or Fabric CAs to generate the organization crypto\n# material.\n\n# By default, the sample network uses cryptogen. Cryptogen is a tool that is\n# meant for development and testing that can quickly create the certificates and keys\n# that can be consumed by a Fabric network. The cryptogen tool consumes a series\n# of configuration files for each organization in the \"organizations/cryptogen\"\n# directory. Cryptogen uses the files to generate the crypto material for each\n# org in the \"organizations\" directory.\n\n# You can also use Fabric CAs to generate the crypto material. CAs sign the certificates\n# and keys that they generate to create a valid root of trust for each organization.\n# The script uses Docker Compose to bring up three CAs, one for each peer organization\n# and the ordering organization. The configuration file for creating the Fabric CA\n# servers are in the \"organizations/fabric-ca\" directory. Within the same directory,\n# the \"registerEnroll.sh\" script uses the Fabric CA client to create the identities,\n# certificates, and MSP folders that are needed to create the test network in the\n# \"organizations/ordererOrganizations\" directory.\n\n# Create Organization crypto material using cryptogen or CAs\nfunction createOrgs() {\n if [ -d \"organizations/peerOrganizations\" ]; then\n rm -Rf organizations/peerOrganizations \u0026\u0026 rm -Rf organizations/ordererOrganizations\n fi\n\n # Create crypto material using cryptogen\n if [ \"$CRYPTO\" == \"cryptogen\" ]; then\n which cryptogen\n if [ \"$?\" -ne 0 ]; then\n fatalln \"cryptogen tool not found. exiting\"\n fi\n infoln \"Generating certificates using cryptogen tool\"\n\n infoln \"Creating Org1 Identities\"\n\n set -x\n cryptogen generate --config=./organizations/cryptogen/crypto-config-org1.yaml --output=\"organizations\"\n res=$?\n { set +x; } 2\u003e/dev/null\n if [ $res -ne 0 ]; then\n fatalln \"Failed to generate certificates...\"\n fi\n\n infoln \"Creating Org2 Identities\"\n\n set -x\n cryptogen generate --config=./organizations/cryptogen/crypto-config-org2.yaml --output=\"organizations\"\n res=$?\n { set +x; } 2\u003e/dev/null\n if [ $res -ne 0 ]; then\n fatalln \"Failed to generate certificates...\"\n fi\n\n infoln \"Creating Orderer Org Identities\"\n\n set -x\n cryptogen generate --config=./organizations/cryptogen/crypto-config-orderer.yaml --output=\"organizations\"\n res=$?\n { set +x; } 2\u003e/dev/null\n if [ $res -ne 0 ]; then\n fatalln \"Failed to generate certificates...\"\n fi\n\n fi\n\n # Create crypto material using cfssl\n if [ \"$CRYPTO\" == \"cfssl\" ]; then\n\n . organizations/cfssl/registerEnroll.sh\n #function_name cert-type CN org\n peer_cert peer peer0.org1.example.com org1\n peer_cert admin Admin@org1.example.com org1\n\n infoln \"Creating Org2 Identities\"\n #function_name cert-type CN org\n peer_cert peer peer0.org2.example.com org2\n peer_cert admin Admin@org2.example.com org2\n\n infoln \"Creating Orderer Org Identities\"\n #function_name cert-type CN \n orderer_cert orderer orderer.example.com\n orderer_cert admin Admin@example.com\n\n fi \n\n # Create crypto material using Fabric CA\n if [ \"$CRYPTO\" == \"Certificate Authorities\" ]; then\n infoln \"Generating certificates using Fabric CA\"\n ${CONTAINER_CLI_COMPOSE} -f compose/$COMPOSE_FILE_CA up -d 2\u003e\u00261\n\n . organizations/fabric-ca/registerEnroll.sh\n\n # Make sure CA files have been created\n while :\n do\n if [ ! -f \"organizations/fabric-ca/org1/tls-cert.pem\" ]; then\n sleep 1\n else\n break\n fi\n done\n\n # Make sure CA service is initialized and can accept requests before making register and enroll calls\n export FABRIC_CA_CLIENT_HOME=${PWD}/organizations/peerOrganizations/org1.example.com/\n COUNTER=0\n rc=1\n while [[ $rc -ne 0 \u0026\u0026 $COUNTER -lt $MAX_RETRY ]]; do\n sleep 1\n set -x\n fabric-ca-client getcainfo -u https://admin:adminpw@localhost:7054 --caname ca-org1 --tls.certfiles \"${PWD}/organizations/fabric-ca/org1/ca-cert.pem\"\n res=$?\n { set +x; } 2\u003e/dev/null\n rc=$res # Update rc\n COUNTER=$((COUNTER + 1))\n done\n\n infoln \"Creating Org1 Identities\"\n\n createOrg1\n\n infoln \"Creating Org2 Identities\"\n\n createOrg2\n\n infoln \"Creating Orderer Org Identities\"\n\n createOrderer\n\n fi\n\n infoln \"Generating CCP files for Org1 and Org2\"\n ./organizations/ccp-generate.sh\n}\n\n# Once you create the organization crypto material, you need to create the\n# genesis block of the application channel.\n\n# The configtxgen tool is used to create the genesis block. Configtxgen consumes a\n# \"configtx.yaml\" file that contains the definitions for the sample network. The\n# genesis block is defined using the \"ChannelUsingRaft\" profile at the bottom\n# of the file. This profile defines an application channel consisting of our two Peer Orgs.\n# The peer and ordering organizations are defined in the \"Profiles\" section at the\n# top of the file. As part of each organization profile, the file points to the\n# location of the MSP directory for each member. This MSP is used to create the channel\n# MSP that defines the root of trust for each organization. In essence, the channel\n# MSP allows the nodes and users to be recognized as network members.\n#\n# If you receive the following warning, it can be safely ignored:\n#\n# [bccsp] GetDefault -\u003e WARN 001 Before using BCCSP, please call InitFactories(). Falling back to bootBCCSP.\n#\n# You can ignore the logs regarding intermediate certs, we are not using them in\n# this crypto implementation.\n\n# After we create the org crypto material and the application channel genesis block,\n# we can now bring up the peers and ordering service. By default, the base\n# file for creating the network is \"docker-compose-test-net.yaml\" in the &#96;&#96;docker&#96;&#96;\n# folder. This file defines the environment variables and file mounts that\n# point the crypto material and genesis block that were created in earlier.\n\n# Bring up the peer and orderer nodes using docker compose.\nfunction networkUp() {\n\n checkPrereqs\n\n # generate artifacts if they don't exist\n if [ ! -d \"organizations/peerOrganizations\" ]; then\n createOrgs\n fi\n\n COMPOSE_FILES=\"-f compose/${COMPOSE_FILE_BASE} -f compose/${CONTAINER_CLI}/${CONTAINER_CLI}-${COMPOSE_FILE_BASE}\"\n\n if [ \"${DATABASE}\" == \"couchdb\" ]; then\n COMPOSE_FILES=\"${COMPOSE_FILES} -f compose/${COMPOSE_FILE_COUCH}\"\n fi\n\n DOCKER_SOCK=\"${DOCKER_SOCK}\" ${CONTAINER_CLI_COMPOSE} ${COMPOSE_FILES} up -d 2\u003e\u00261\n\n $CONTAINER_CLI ps -a\n if [ $? -ne 0 ]; then\n fatalln \"Unable to start network\"\n fi\n}\n\n# call the script to create the channel, join the peers of org1 and org2,\n# and then update the anchor peers for each organization\nfunction createChannel() {\n # Bring up the network if it is not already up.\n bringUpNetwork=\"false\"\n\n local bft_true=$1\n\n if ! $CONTAINER_CLI info \u003e /dev/null 2\u003e\u00261 ; then\n fatalln \"$CONTAINER_CLI network is required to be running to create a channel\"\n fi\n\n # check if all containers are present\n CONTAINERS=($($CONTAINER_CLI ps | grep hyperledger/ | awk '{print $2}'))\n len=$(echo ${#CONTAINERS[@]})\n\n if [[ $len -ge 4 ]] \u0026\u0026 [[ ! -d \"organizations/peerOrganizations\" ]]; then\n echo \"Bringing network down to sync certs with containers\"\n networkDown\n fi\n\n [[ $len -lt 4 ]] || [[ ! -d \"organizations/peerOrganizations\" ]] \u0026\u0026 bringUpNetwork=\"true\" || echo \"Network Running Already\"\n\n if [ $bringUpNetwork == \"true\" ]; then\n infoln \"Bringing up network\"\n networkUp\n fi\n\n # now run the script that creates a channel. This script uses configtxgen once\n # to create the channel creation transaction and the anchor peer updates.\n scripts/createChannel.sh $CHANNEL_NAME $CLI_DELAY $MAX_RETRY $VERBOSE $bft_true\n}\n\n## Call the script to deploy a chaincode to the channel\nfunction deployCC() {\n scripts/deployCC.sh $CHANNEL_NAME $CC_NAME $CC_SRC_PATH $CC_SRC_LANGUAGE $CC_VERSION $CC_SEQUENCE $CC_INIT_FCN $CC_END_POLICY $CC_COLL_CONFIG $CLI_DELAY $MAX_RETRY $VERBOSE\n\n if [ $? -ne 0 ]; then\n fatalln \"Deploying chaincode failed\"\n fi\n}\n\n## Call the script to deploy a chaincode to the channel\nfunction deployCCAAS() {\n scripts/deployCCAAS.sh $CHANNEL_NAME $CC_NAME $CC_SRC_PATH $CCAAS_DOCKER_RUN $CC_VERSION $CC_SEQUENCE $CC_INIT_FCN $CC_END_POLICY $CC_COLL_CONFIG $CLI_DELAY $MAX_RETRY $VERBOSE $CCAAS_DOCKER_RUN\n\n if [ $? -ne 0 ]; then\n fatalln \"Deploying chaincode-as-a-service failed\"\n fi\n}\n\n## Call the script to package the chaincode\nfunction packageChaincode() {\n\n infoln \"Packaging chaincode\"\n\n scripts/packageCC.sh $CC_NAME $CC_SRC_PATH $CC_SRC_LANGUAGE $CC_VERSION true\n\n if [ $? -ne 0 ]; then\n fatalln \"Packaging the chaincode failed\"\n fi\n\n}\n\n## Call the script to list installed and committed chaincode on a peer\nfunction listChaincode() {\n\n export FABRIC_CFG_PATH=${PWD}/../config\n\n . scripts/envVar.sh\n . scripts/ccutils.sh\n\n setGlobals $ORG\n\n println\n queryInstalledOnPeer\n println\n\n listAllCommitted\n\n}\n\n## Call the script to invoke \nfunction invokeChaincode() {\n\n export FABRIC_CFG_PATH=${PWD}/../config\n\n . scripts/envVar.sh\n . scripts/ccutils.sh\n\n setGlobals $ORG\n\n chaincodeInvoke $ORG $CHANNEL_NAME $CC_NAME $CC_INVOKE_CONSTRUCTOR\n\n}\n\n## Call the script to query chaincode \nfunction queryChaincode() {\n\n export FABRIC_CFG_PATH=${PWD}/../config\n \n . scripts/envVar.sh\n . scripts/ccutils.sh\n\n setGlobals $ORG\n\n chaincodeQuery $ORG $CHANNEL_NAME $CC_NAME $CC_QUERY_CONSTRUCTOR\n\n}\n\n# Tear down running network\nfunction networkDown() {\n local temp_compose=$COMPOSE_FILE_BASE\n COMPOSE_FILE_BASE=compose-bft-test-net.yaml\n COMPOSE_BASE_FILES=\"-f compose/${COMPOSE_FILE_BASE} -f compose/${CONTAINER_CLI}/${CONTAINER_CLI}-${COMPOSE_FILE_BASE}\"\n COMPOSE_COUCH_FILES=\"-f compose/${COMPOSE_FILE_COUCH}\"\n COMPOSE_CA_FILES=\"-f compose/${COMPOSE_FILE_CA}\"\n COMPOSE_FILES=\"${COMPOSE_BASE_FILES} ${COMPOSE_COUCH_FILES} ${COMPOSE_CA_FILES}\"\n\n # stop org3 containers also in addition to org1 and org2, in case we were running sample to add org3\n COMPOSE_ORG3_BASE_FILES=\"-f addOrg3/compose/${COMPOSE_FILE_ORG3_BASE} -f addOrg3/compose/${CONTAINER_CLI}/${CONTAINER_CLI}-${COMPOSE_FILE_ORG3_BASE}\"\n COMPOSE_ORG3_COUCH_FILES=\"-f addOrg3/compose/${COMPOSE_FILE_ORG3_COUCH} -f addOrg3/compose/${CONTAINER_CLI}/${CONTAINER_CLI}-${COMPOSE_FILE_ORG3_COUCH}\"\n COMPOSE_ORG3_CA_FILES=\"-f addOrg3/compose/${COMPOSE_FILE_ORG3_CA} -f addOrg3/compose/${CONTAINER_CLI}/${CONTAINER_CLI}-${COMPOSE_FILE_ORG3_CA}\"\n COMPOSE_ORG3_FILES=\"${COMPOSE_ORG3_BASE_FILES} ${COMPOSE_ORG3_COUCH_FILES} ${COMPOSE_ORG3_CA_FILES}\"\n\n if [ \"${CONTAINER_CLI}\" == \"docker\" ]; then\n DOCKER_SOCK=$DOCKER_SOCK ${CONTAINER_CLI_COMPOSE} ${COMPOSE_FILES} ${COMPOSE_ORG3_FILES} down --volumes --remove-orphans\n elif [ \"${CONTAINER_CLI}\" == \"podman\" ]; then\n ${CONTAINER_CLI_COMPOSE} ${COMPOSE_FILES} ${COMPOSE_ORG3_FILES} down --volumes\n else\n fatalln \"Container CLI ${CONTAINER_CLI} not supported\"\n fi\n\n COMPOSE_FILE_BASE=$temp_compose\n\n # Don't remove the generated artifacts -- note, the ledgers are always removed\n if [ \"$MODE\" != \"restart\" ]; then\n # Bring down the network, deleting the volumes\n ${CONTAINER_CLI} volume rm docker_orderer.example.com docker_peer0.org1.example.com docker_peer0.org2.example.com\n #Cleanup the chaincode containers\n clearContainers\n #Cleanup images\n removeUnwantedImages\n # remove orderer block and other channel configuration transactions and certs\n ${CONTAINER_CLI} run --rm -v \"$(pwd):/data\" busybox sh -c 'cd /data \u0026\u0026 rm -rf system-genesis-block/*.block organizations/peerOrganizations organizations/ordererOrganizations'\n ## remove fabric ca artifacts\n ${CONTAINER_CLI} run --rm -v \"$(pwd):/data\" busybox sh -c 'cd /data \u0026\u0026 rm -rf organizations/fabric-ca/org1/msp organizations/fabric-ca/org1/tls-cert.pem organizations/fabric-ca/org1/ca-cert.pem organizations/fabric-ca/org1/IssuerPublicKey organizations/fabric-ca/org1/IssuerRevocationPublicKey organizations/fabric-ca/org1/fabric-ca-server.db'\n ${CONTAINER_CLI} run --rm -v \"$(pwd):/data\" busybox sh -c 'cd /data \u0026\u0026 rm -rf organizations/fabric-ca/org2/msp organizations/fabric-ca/org2/tls-cert.pem organizations/fabric-ca/org2/ca-cert.pem organizations/fabric-ca/org2/IssuerPublicKey organizations/fabric-ca/org2/IssuerRevocationPublicKey organizations/fabric-ca/org2/fabric-ca-server.db'\n ${CONTAINER_CLI} run --rm -v \"$(pwd):/data\" busybox sh -c 'cd /data \u0026\u0026 rm -rf organizations/fabric-ca/ordererOrg/msp organizations/fabric-ca/ordererOrg/tls-cert.pem organizations/fabric-ca/ordererOrg/ca-cert.pem organizations/fabric-ca/ordererOrg/IssuerPublicKey organizations/fabric-ca/ordererOrg/IssuerRevocationPublicKey organizations/fabric-ca/ordererOrg/fabric-ca-server.db'\n ${CONTAINER_CLI} run --rm -v \"$(pwd):/data\" busybox sh -c 'cd /data \u0026\u0026 rm -rf addOrg3/fabric-ca/org3/msp addOrg3/fabric-ca/org3/tls-cert.pem addOrg3/fabric-ca/org3/ca-cert.pem addOrg3/fabric-ca/org3/IssuerPublicKey addOrg3/fabric-ca/org3/IssuerRevocationPublicKey addOrg3/fabric-ca/org3/fabric-ca-server.db'\n # remove channel and script artifacts\n ${CONTAINER_CLI} run --rm -v \"$(pwd):/data\" busybox sh -c 'cd /data \u0026\u0026 rm -rf channel-artifacts log.txt *.tar.gz'\n fi\n}\n\n. ./network.config\n\n# use this as the default docker-compose yaml definition\nCOMPOSE_FILE_BASE=compose-test-net.yaml\n# docker-compose.yaml file if you are using couchdb\nCOMPOSE_FILE_COUCH=compose-couch.yaml\n# certificate authorities compose file\nCOMPOSE_FILE_CA=compose-ca.yaml\n# use this as the default docker-compose yaml definition for org3\nCOMPOSE_FILE_ORG3_BASE=compose-org3.yaml\n# use this as the docker compose couch file for org3\nCOMPOSE_FILE_ORG3_COUCH=compose-couch-org3.yaml\n# certificate authorities compose file\nCOMPOSE_FILE_ORG3_CA=compose-ca-org3.yaml\n#\n\n# Get docker sock path from environment variable\nSOCK=\"${DOCKER_HOST:-/var/run/docker.sock}\"\nDOCKER_SOCK=\"${SOCK##unix://}\"\n\n# BFT activated flag\nBFT=0\n\n# Parse commandline args\n\n## Parse mode\nif [[ $# -lt 1 ]] ; then\n printHelp\n exit 0\nelse\n MODE=$1\n shift\nfi\n\n## if no parameters are passed, show the help for cc\nif [ \"$MODE\" == \"cc\" ] \u0026\u0026 [[ $# -lt 1 ]]; then\n printHelp $MODE\n exit 0\nfi\n\n# parse subcommands if used\nif [[ $# -ge 1 ]] ; then\n key=\"$1\"\n # check for the createChannel subcommand\n if [[ \"$key\" == \"createChannel\" ]]; then\n export MODE=\"createChannel\"\n shift\n # check for the cc command\n elif [[ \"$MODE\" == \"cc\" ]]; then\n if [ \"$1\" != \"-h\" ]; then\n export SUBCOMMAND=$key\n shift\n fi\n fi\nfi\n\n# parse flags\n\nwhile [[ $# -ge 1 ]] ; do\n key=\"$1\"\n case $key in\n -h )\n printHelp $MODE\n exit 0\n ;;\n -c )\n CHANNEL_NAME=\"$2\"\n shift\n ;;\n -bft )\n BFT=1\n ;;\n -ca )\n CRYPTO=\"Certificate Authorities\"\n ;;\n -cfssl )\n CRYPTO=\"cfssl\"\n ;;\n -r )\n MAX_RETRY=\"$2\"\n shift\n ;;\n -d )\n CLI_DELAY=\"$2\"\n shift\n ;;\n -s )\n DATABASE=\"$2\"\n shift\n ;;\n -ccl )\n CC_SRC_LANGUAGE=\"$2\"\n shift\n ;;\n -ccn )\n CC_NAME=\"$2\"\n shift\n ;;\n -ccv )\n CC_VERSION=\"$2\"\n shift\n ;;\n -ccs )\n CC_SEQUENCE=\"$2\"\n shift\n ;;\n -ccp )\n CC_SRC_PATH=\"$2\"\n shift\n ;;\n -ccep )\n CC_END_POLICY=\"$2\"\n shift\n ;;\n -cccg )\n CC_COLL_CONFIG=\"$2\"\n shift\n ;;\n -cci )\n CC_INIT_FCN=\"$2\"\n shift\n ;;\n -ccaasdocker )\n CCAAS_DOCKER_RUN=\"$2\"\n shift\n ;;\n -verbose )\n VERBOSE=true\n ;;\n -org )\n ORG=\"$2\"\n shift\n ;;\n -i )\n IMAGETAG=\"$2\"\n shift\n ;;\n -cai )\n CA_IMAGETAG=\"$2\"\n shift\n ;;\n -ccic )\n CC_INVOKE_CONSTRUCTOR=\"$2\"\n shift\n ;;\n -ccqc )\n CC_QUERY_CONSTRUCTOR=\"$2\"\n shift\n ;; \n * )\n errorln \"Unknown flag: $key\"\n printHelp\n exit 1\n ;;\n esac\n shift\ndone\n\nif [ $BFT -eq 1 ]; then\n export FABRIC_CFG_PATH=${PWD}/bft-config\n COMPOSE_FILE_BASE=compose-bft-test-net.yaml\nfi\n\n# Are we generating crypto material with this command?\nif [ ! -d \"organizations/peerOrganizations\" ]; then\n CRYPTO_MODE=\"with crypto from '${CRYPTO}'\"\nelse\n CRYPTO_MODE=\"\"\nfi\n\n# Determine mode of operation and printing out what we asked for\nif [ \"$MODE\" == \"prereq\" ]; then\n infoln \"Installing binaries and fabric images. Fabric Version: ${IMAGETAG} Fabric CA Version: ${CA_IMAGETAG}\"\n installPrereqs\nelif [ \"$MODE\" == \"up\" ]; then\n infoln \"Starting nodes with CLI timeout of '${MAX_RETRY}' tries and CLI delay of '${CLI_DELAY}' seconds and using database '${DATABASE}' ${CRYPTO_MODE}\"\n networkUp\nelif [ \"$MODE\" == \"createChannel\" ]; then\n infoln \"Creating channel '${CHANNEL_NAME}'.\"\n infoln \"If network is not up, starting nodes with CLI timeout of '${MAX_RETRY}' tries and CLI delay of '${CLI_DELAY}' seconds and using database '${DATABASE} ${CRYPTO_MODE}\"\n createChannel $BFT\nelif [ \"$MODE\" == \"down\" ]; then\n infoln \"Stopping network\"\n networkDown\nelif [ \"$MODE\" == \"restart\" ]; then\n infoln \"Restarting network\"\n networkDown\n networkUp\nelif [ \"$MODE\" == \"deployCC\" ]; then\n infoln \"deploying chaincode on channel '${CHANNEL_NAME}'\"\n deployCC\nelif [ \"$MODE\" == \"deployCCAAS\" ]; then\n infoln \"deploying chaincode-as-a-service on channel '${CHANNEL_NAME}'\"\n deployCCAAS\nelif [ \"$MODE\" == \"cc\" ] \u0026\u0026 [ \"$SUBCOMMAND\" == \"package\" ]; then\n packageChaincode\nelif [ \"$MODE\" == \"cc\" ] \u0026\u0026 [ \"$SUBCOMMAND\" == \"list\" ]; then\n listChaincode\nelif [ \"$MODE\" == \"cc\" ] \u0026\u0026 [ \"$SUBCOMMAND\" == \"invoke\" ]; then\n invokeChaincode\nelif [ \"$MODE\" == \"cc\" ] \u0026\u0026 [ \"$SUBCOMMAND\" == \"query\" ]; then\n queryChaincode\nelse\n printHelp\n exit 1\nfi\n",

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
fabric-chaincode-node may not be listed in dependencies but could originate from fabric-shim peer or be included in the docker image. The project uses ^2.2.0 which aligns with backend fabric-network. I'll fix the module.exports for the contracts array.

For the step-by-step guide, I'll include:
- Prerequisites: Docker Desktop, Git, curl, jq (mac: brew install jq)
- Install Fabric samples + binaries per https://hyperledger-fabric.readthedocs.io/en/latest/install.html
- Clone fabric-samples at a stable tag matching binaries (e.g. v2.5.x)
- cd test-network && ./network.sh down && ./network.sh up createChannel -c mychannel -ca npm install must be run in chaincode/sim-registry before deployment, though the deploy script may handle npm install within the container.

The deployCC command for JavaScript chaincodes typically runs npm install during the docker build process.

- Deploy chaincode using: ./network.sh deployCC -ccn simregistry -ccp ABSOLUTE_PATH_TO/explora-app-ui/chaincode/sim-registry -ccl javascript -c mychannel
- Optionally initialize with: -cci initLedger
- If CC_INIT_FCN is set to NA, the init invoke may be skipped, which is suitable for recordAudit-only chaincodes.</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

Here is a **step-by-step path** tailored to this repo: channel **`mychannel`**, chaincode name **`simregistry`**, transaction **`recordAudit`**, and backend env vars as in `backend/.env.example`.

---

### Step 0 — Prerequisites (one machine, e.g. macOS)

1. **Docker Desktop** running (enough RAM; 8 GB+ is safer).
2. **Git**, **curl**, **jq** (e.g. `brew install jq`).
3. Keep **Fabric samples, peer binaries, and Docker images on the same release family** (e.g. all 2.5.x or all 2.2.x). Your backend uses **`fabric-network` ~2.2**; Fabric 2.5 samples usually still work for class projects, but if anything misbehaves, use the **fabric-samples `release-2.2`** branch and matching [install instructions](https://hyperledger-fabric.readthedocs.io/en/release-2.2/install.html).

---

### Step 1 — Install Fabric binaries and Docker images

From the [official install guide](https://hyperledger-fabric.readthedocs.io/en/latest/install.html), or the shortcut in fabric-samples:

```bash
cd ~
curl -sSLO https://raw.githubusercontent.com/hyperledger/fabric/main/scripts/install-fabric.sh
chmod +x install-fabric.sh
./install-fabric.sh docker samples binary
```

That gives you **`fabric-samples`**, **`fabric-samples/bin`** (`peer`, `configtxgen`, `fabric-ca-client`, …), and pulls the Hyperledger **Docker images**.

Add binaries to your PATH when working in the test network (the samples assume this):

```bash
export PATH="$HOME/fabric-samples/bin:$PATH"
```

---

### Step 2 — Start the test network + channel + CAs

```bash
cd ~/fabric-samples/test-network
./network.sh down
./network.sh up createChannel -c mychannel -ca
```

- **`-ca`** uses Fabric CAs (good for enrolling an app identity later).
- Default channel name is **`mychannel`**, which matches your backend default.

Wait until the script finishes without errors.

---

### Step 3 — Install chaincode dependencies (your repo)

```bash
cd "/Users/muhammedabdurehman/Desktop/FYP/3rd Presentation/explora-app-ui/chaincode/sim-registry"
npm install
```

(`deployCC` will still rebuild inside Docker, but a clean local `node_modules` avoids surprises.)

---

### Step 4 — Deploy **`sim-registry`** as **`simregistry`**

From **`fabric-samples/test-network`**, use an **absolute** path to your chaincode folder:

```bash
cd ~/fabric-samples/test-network

CC_PATH="/Users/muhammedabdurehman/Desktop/FYP/3rd Presentation/explora-app-ui/chaincode/sim-registry"

./network.sh deployCC -c mychannel -ccn simregistry -ccp "$CC_PATH" -ccl javascript
```

- **`-ccn simregistry`** must match **`FABRIC_CHAINCODE_NAME`** (default in your backend).
- **`-ccl javascript`** selects Node chaincode packaging.

If the script complains about **jq**, install it and rerun from Step 4.

---

### Step 5 — Smoke-test on the ledger (optional)

With org1 peer env set (from fabric-samples docs, usually after `export $(./setOrgEnv.sh org1)` or the equivalent in your samples version), invoke **`recordAudit`**:

```bash
peer chaincode invoke \
  -C mychannel \
  -n simregistry \
  -c '{"function":"recordAudit","Args":["{\"action\":\"ping\",\"source\":\"cli\"}"]}'
```

(Exact **TLS / MSP env** flags depend on your samples version; if `peer` errors, use the **invoke** snippet from the test-network README for your clone.)

---

### Step 6 — Point the Node backend at Fabric

1. **Connection profile** (generated when the network came up):

   Typical location:

   `~/fabric-samples/test-network/organizations/peerOrganizations/org1.example.com/connection-org1.json`

2. **Client identity (MSP folder)** — fastest for a **demo** (Org1 admin — **not** for real production):

   `~/fabric-samples/test-network/organizations/peerOrganizations/org1.example.com/users/Admin@org1.example.com/msp`

   That folder contains **`signcerts/cert.pem`** and **`keystore/`**, which `fabric-gateway.js` expects when using **`FABRIC_USER_CREDENTIALS_DIR`**.

3. In **`backend/.env`** (or your shell), set:

```bash
FABRIC_ENABLED=true
FABRIC_CONNECTION_PROFILE_PATH=/full/path/to/connection-org1.json
FABRIC_USER_CREDENTIALS_DIR=/full/path/to/users/Admin@org1.example.com/msp
FABRIC_IDENTITY_LABEL=appUser
```

**Important:** The wallet label **`FABRIC_IDENTITY_LABEL`** must match an identity **stored in the wallet**. The in-memory wallet is built from the MSP folder and uses the **filename** / identity label you pass to `wallet.put`. Your gateway uses label **`appUser`** by default but puts the cert under that label from the MSP folder — check `fabric-gateway.js`: it uses `identityLabel` from env default **`appUser`** and `buildWalletFromCredentialsDir` puts one identity with that label. So the **label is `appUser`**, not `Admin@org1` — the code reads MSP material and stores it as `appUser`. So **`FABRIC_IDENTITY_LABEL=appUser`** is correct with **`FABRIC_USER_CREDENTIALS_DIR`** pointing at Admin’s MSP (it loads that cert/key and labels it `appUser`). Good.

4. **`FABRIC_DISCOVERY_AS_LOCALHOST=true`** when the peer’s discovery addresses are **localhost** (default test-network on the same machine).

5. Run the backend from **`backend/`** with that `.env` and trigger a flow that calls **`submitToBlockchain`** (e.g. SIM registration). You should see a Fabric commit log, not “skipped”.

---

### Step 7 — Teardown when you are done

```bash
cd ~/fabric-samples/test-network
./network.sh down
```

---

### Code change already applied in your repo

JavaScript chaincode must register contracts the way **fabric-samples** does. Your file only had `module.exports = SIMRegistryContract`, which can break **`fabric-chaincode-node`**. It is now:

```293:296:chaincode/sim-registry/index.js
// fabric-chaincode-node expects `contracts` (same pattern as fabric-samples JS chaincode).
module.exports.SIMRegistryContract = SIMRegistryContract;
module.exports.contracts = [SIMRegistryContract];
```

---

### If something fails

| Symptom | What to check |
|--------|----------------|
| `peer: command not found` | `export PATH=.../fabric-samples/bin:$PATH` |
| Binary vs Docker image version mismatch | Re-run `./install-fabric.sh` for one version; use matching **fabric-samples** branch |
| Chaincode build fails | Node in chaincode `package.json` / Docker image; run `npm install` in `chaincode/sim-registry` |
| Backend TLS / discovery errors | `FABRIC_DISCOVERY_AS_LOCALHOST`, correct paths in `connection-org1.json`, peer/orderer up |
| Vercel | Fabric stays **off** unless **`FABRIC_ENABLED=true`**; real Fabric is almost always **a VM or on-prem peer**, not serverless |

If you tell me your OS, Fabric version from `peer version`, and the **first error line** from `deployCC`, we can narrow the next command (e.g. sequence bump or org TLS).

---

- Bash being removed or relocated on newer macOS systems where zsh is the default shell
- The script expecting bash at `/bin/bash`, which may not be accessible in some environments

This is unusual on macOS since bash typically exists at `/bin/bash`. The error "env: bash: No such file or directory" indicates that the `env` command cannot locate the `bash` executable in the current PATH. This can occur if:

- Bash is installed in a non-standard location (like `/usr/local/bin`) but not in PATH
- The script uses a shebang like `#!/usr/bin/env bash` but bash isn't available in the container environment
- PATH is incorrectly configured or missing essential directories

The issue may also stem from using `env bash` in a container context where bash is not installed or accessible, even though the user runs the script locally on macOS. The error "env: bash: No such file or directory" occurs when the system cannot locate the bash interpreter. This can happen if bash is installed in a non-standard location or if the PATH is misconfigured.

Solutions include:
- Installing bash via Homebrew (`brew install bash`) and ensuring PATH includes it
- Changing the shebang line in scripts from `#!/usr/bin/env bash` to `#!/bin/bash`
- Running scripts explicitly with `bash ./script.sh`

For immediate use, run:
```bash
bash ./network.sh down
bash ./network.sh up
``` If `/bin/bash` exists, running `bash ./network.sh` should work even if the shebang fails. When the shebang is `#!/usr/bin/env bash`, the kernel uses it, which then looks for bash in PATH. If PATH doesn't include `/bin` or bash was removed, this can fail.

On macOS 15+, environments may be stripped, and it's more common that the user's interactive shell has a correct PATH, but the script runs in a context with a minimal PATH. This explains why running `./network.sh` directly might fail, while explicitly calling `bash ./network.sh` works.</details></think>

ls -la /bin/bash

  ```

### 3. If there is no `bash`

Install one and put it on `PATH`:

```bash
brew install bash
```

Then either use `bash ./network.sh ...` or ensure `$(brew --prefix)/bin` is in your `PATH` for login shells.

### 4. If you use `fish` or another non-POSIX shell

Still run the scripts with **`bash`** explicitly as in step 1.

---

**Summary:** Run `bash ./network.sh down` and `bash ./network.sh up createChannel -c mychannel -ca`. If `command -v bash` prints nothing, install `bash` (e.g. Homebrew) or fix `PATH` so `/bin/bash` is visible.

_**Agent (default)**_

The problem is this line:

```bash
export PATH="fabric-samples/bin:/Users/muhammedabdurehman/Desktop/FYP/3rd Presentation/explora-app-ui"
```

That **replaces** your whole `PATH` with only those two entries. Standard locations like **`/bin` and `/usr/bin`** are gone, so **`bash` is no longer on `PATH`**. The `network.sh` shebang runs `/usr/bin/env bash`, `env` looks up `bash` in `PATH`, finds nothing → **`env: bash: No such file or directory`**.

### Fix

**1. Restore a normal `PATH` in this terminal** (open a new tab/window, or reset):

```bash
export PATH="/usr/local/bin:/usr/bin:/bin:/usr/sbin:/sbin:$PATH"
```

(or just close the terminal and open a new one.)

**2. Prepend** Fabric + your project; **do not drop `$PATH`**:

```bash
export PATH="$HOME/fabric-samples/bin:$PATH"
```

(Only add the explora path if you actually need those binaries on `PATH`; it’s not required for `network.sh`.)

**3. Run the network again:**

```bash
cd ~/fabric-samples/test-network
./network.sh down
./network.sh up createChannel -c mychannel -ca
```

**Rule of thumb:** always use **`...:$PATH`** at the end when extending `PATH`, unless you really intend to replace the whole system path.

You’re past the `bash` / `PATH` issue. What’s failing now is **Docker**, in two separate ways.

---

### 1. `unknown shorthand flag: 'f' in -f`

That usually means the script is effectively running **`docker -f …`** instead of **`docker compose -f …`** (Compose not available the way the test network expects).

**Check:**

```bash
docker version
docker compose version
```

- If **`docker compose version`** fails, install/enable **Docker Compose v2** (simplest: install or update **[Docker Desktop for Mac](https://www.docker.com/products/docker-desktop/)** and start it).
- If you use **Colima / OrbStack**, follow their docs to install the **Compose plugin** or standalone **`docker-compose`**, so either `docker compose` or `docker-compose` works.

The Fabric `network.sh` picks **`docker-compose`** if that binary exists, otherwise **`docker compose`**. One of those must work.

---

### 2. `docker-credential-desktop: executable file not found in $PATH`

Docker is trying to use the **Desktop credential helper**, but that binary isn’t on your `PATH` (common if the CLI is installed without the full app, or the app isn’t running).

**Option A — Recommended:** Start **Docker Desktop** from Applications and wait until it’s fully up, then try again.

**Option B — Add Docker’s CLI to PATH** (typical on Mac):

```bash
export PATH="/Applications/Docker.app/Contents/Resources/bin:$PATH"
```

(You can add that line to `~/.zshrc` if it fixes it.)

**Option C — Turn off the missing helper** (dev machines only; relaxes credential storage):

1. Open `~/.docker/config.json`.
2. Remove **`"credsStore": "desktop"`** (or **`"credStore"`** pointing at desktop), **or** set **`"credsStore": ""`** if the file allows it.

Then retry:

```bash
cd ~/fabric-samples/test-network
./network.sh down
./network.sh up createChannel -c mychannel -ca
```

---

### Quick checklist

| Check | Command / action |
|--------|-------------------|
| Daemon running | Docker Desktop running (whale icon idle) |
| Compose v2 | `docker compose version` succeeds |
| Credential helper | Docker Desktop started **or** PATH **or** edit `config.json` |

After **`docker compose version`** and **`docker run hello-world`** both work, `network.sh` should get past the `-f` and `busybox` steps. If anything still errors, paste the output of **`docker compose version`** and **`which docker`**.

- It should follow the symlink to access the actual directory contents instead This is a known issue where tar walkers fail when the root path is a symlink, treating it as a file instead of traversing its contents.

_**Agent (default)**_

`peer lifecycle chaincode package` is choking because **`~/sim-registry-chaincode` is a symlink** to a directory. The packager walks the tree and ends up trying to treat that root entry like a normal file (`read …: is a directory`).

### Fix: use a real directory (copy), not a symlink

```bash
rm -f ~/sim-registry-chaincode

cp -R "/Users/muhammedabdurehman/Desktop/FYP/3rd Presentation/explora-app-ui/chaincode/sim-registry" \
  ~/sim-registry-chaincode

cd ~/fabric-samples/test-network
./network.sh deployCC -c mychannel -ccn simregistry -ccp "$HOME/sim-registry-chaincode" -ccl javascript
```

Confirm it’s a directory, not a link:

```

You want **`d`** at the start, not **`l`**.

If packaging still fails, from that copy run **`npm install`** then retry `deployCC`:

```bash
cd ~/sim-registry-chaincode && npm install
cd ~/fabric-samples/test-network && ./network.sh deployCC -c mychannel -ccn simregistry -ccp "$HOME/sim-registry-chaincode" -ccl javascript
```

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The user is attempting to run `peer chaincode invoke` from the host without configuring the required Fabric peer environment variables. The peer CLI requires three key environment variables:
- `FABRIC_CFG_PATH` pointing to the core.yaml location (typically fabric-samples/config)
- MSP configuration for the identity
- TLS options for peer connection

The fabric-samples test-network usually handles this through scripts like `setOrgEnv.sh` or by running commands inside the cli container.

Standard approach from Hyperledger docs:
- Set `FABRIC_CFG_PATH` to the config directory
- Enable TLS with `CORE_PEER_TLS_ENABLED=true`
- Set local MSP ID with `CORE_PEER_LOCALMSPID="Org1MSP"` The environment variables `CORE_PEER_TLS_ROOTCERT_FILE`, `CORE_PEER_MSPCONFIGPATH`, and `CORE_PEER_ADDRESS` need to be set to configure the peer connection properly.

The error message indicates that `FABRIC_CFG_PATH` is defaulting to the current directory, but `core.yaml` is missing from `/Users/.../fabric-samples/test-network`.

To resolve this:
- Set `FABRIC_CFG_PATH` to point to the config directory containing `core.yaml`
- Ensure `core.yaml` exists in that location
- Verify TLS settings match the peer's configuration

The peer CLI requires these environment variables for proper operation:
- `CORE_PEER_TLS_ROOTCERT_FILE`: Path to CA certificate
- `CORE_PEER_MSPCONFIGPATH`: Path to MSP configuration
- `CORE_PEER_ADDRESS`: Peer address for connection
- `FABRIC_CFG_PATH`: Path to core.yaml configuration file</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

`peer` is running without a **Fabric core config**. It defaults to the current directory (`test-network`), where there is **no `core.yaml`**, so you get **Config File "core" Not Found**.

### 1. Point `peer` at the samples config (required)

From **`~/fabric-samples/test-network`**:

```bash
export FABRIC_CFG_PATH="$HOME/fabric-samples/config"
```

(That directory should contain **`core.yaml`**. If your clone differs, use the folder that actually has `core.yaml`, often `fabric-samples/config`.)

### 2. Set Org1 peer + TLS + identity (required for invoke)

Still in **`~/fabric-samples/test-network`**:

```bash
export CORE_PEER_TLS_ENABLED=true
export CORE_PEER_LOCALMSPID="Org1MSP"
export CORE_PEER_TLS_ROOTCERT_FILE="$PWD/organizations/peerOrganizations/org1.example.com/peers/peer0.org1.example.com/tls/ca.crt"
export CORE_PEER_MSPCONFIGPATH="$PWD/organizations/peerOrganizations/org1.example.com/users/Admin@org1.example.com/msp"
export CORE_PEER_ADDRESS=localhost:7051
```

### 3. Invoke again

```bash
peer chaincode invoke \
  -o localhost:7050 \
  --ordererTLSHostnameOverride orderer.example.com \
  --tls \
  --cafile "$PWD/organizations/ordererOrganizations/example.com/orderers/orderer.example.com/msp/tlscacerts/tlsca.example.com-cert.pem" \
  -C mychannel \
  -n simregistry \
  --peerAddresses localhost:7051 \
  --tlsRootCertFiles "$PWD/organizations/peerOrganizations/org1.example.com/peers/peer0.org1.example.com/tls/ca.crt" \
  -c '{"function":"recordAudit","Args":["{\"action\":\"ping\",\"source\":\"cli\"}"]}'
```

Orderer hostname/port can differ slightly by Fabric samples version; if this errors, your clone may ship **`setOrgEnv.sh`** — use it and then the shorter invoke from the test-network README:

```bash
# if present:
source ./setOrgEnv.sh org1
peer chaincode invoke ...
```

**Summary:** Set **`FABRIC_CFG_PATH`** to the directory that contains **`core.yaml`**, then set **`CORE_PEER_*`** (and orderer/TLS flags as above) so `peer` knows how to reach the network.
